profile.behavior: Often inconspicuous in low, shrubby cover; males give a rich warbling song from exposed perches. Forages on the ground and in low vegetation, and commonly flicks or twitches its tail sideways.
field_marks: Stocky, full-bodied songbird with a very large triangular bill.
Adult male is deep blue overall, often with a small black mask before the eye.
Bill is bicolored: dark upper mandible and silvery lower mandible.
Two conspicuous wingbars, with a chestnut upper bar and paler lower bar.
Dark, rounded wings and a fairly long dark tail; tail may be flicked sideways.
Females and immature birds are warm cinnamon-brown, with wingbars and often bluish tail or scattered blue feathers.
profile.habitat: Semi-open, shrubby vegetation: brushy fields, hedgerows, woodland edges, young regrowth, thickets, and open weedy ground; also uses shrubby growth near water in drier landscapes.

sources.title: Blue Grosbeak Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Species identity is correct: the illustrated adult male shows the diagnostic stocky/full-bodied form, very large triangular black-and-silver bill, small black facial mask, deep blue plumage, chestnut upper wingbar, paler lower wingbar, dark rounded wing, and long dark tail seen in the supplied references and verified sources.
Visible anatomy is coherent: one head, one beak, two wings (one largely occluded in the posed bird), two legs with feet, and one tail; no duplicated or malformed body parts are evident.

All substantive plate text is supported by the checked sources, including the scientific name, Cardinalidae placement, Audubon measurement ranges, habitat, behavior, bill coloration, wingbars, and female/immature coloration.
Concrete presentation issue: the decorative inch ruler omits the numeral 6 between 5 and 7. It does not create a material species or textual error.
